ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry for kumquat and lemon drink; closest parent 'fruit juice drink' used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fruit and vegetable juices and nectars
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to kumquat-lemon drink; A00LC (Fruit and vegetable juices and nectars) used as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226365003 'Fruit drink' used as closest parent; no specific kumquat-lemon drink code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (one cup or glass), as typically served at Singaporean Chinese beverage stalls and restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Kumquats, raw (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168162) and Lemon juice, raw (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 167747); blended estimate for kumquat-lemon drink with water and sugar
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for kumquat-lemon drink in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for raw kumquats (FDC 168162) and raw lemon juice (FDC 167747), blended in approximate proportions typical of a Singaporean kumquat-lemon drink: ~30% kumquat pulp/juice, ~10% lemon juice, ~55% water, ~5% sugar. Per-100g values represent the ready-to-drink beverage.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of citrus fruit juices and fruit-based beverages; analogous estimate based on lemon juice and kumquat fruit GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for kumquat-lemon drink. GI estimated at 40 (low category) based on University of Sydney GI Database data for citrus fruit juices (lemon juice GI ~20-25, orange juice GI ~50) and the high organic acid and fibre content of kumquats which suppresses glycemic response. Weighted estimate accounting for added sugar content places composite GI at approximately 40. Glycemic load per 250 ml serving = (40 × 9.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 9.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~5 mg per 250 ml serving) and moderate potassium (~213 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Kumquat and lemon drink is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its very low sodium content and moderate potassium contribution, which supports healthy blood pressure regulation; however, added sugar should be minimised or substituted with a low-calorie sweetener.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar and natural fruit sugars contribute to glycemic load (~9.2 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume kumquat and lemon drink in moderation; requesting reduced or no added sugar and limiting portion to 150–200 ml can help manage postprandial blood glucose, given the estimated low GI of approximately 40 but moderate glycemic load per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat, high vitamin C (~45 mg per serving), and antioxidant flavonoids from kumquat peel
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kumquat and lemon drink is generally favourable for cardiovascular health due to its negligible saturated fat, low sodium, and meaningful vitamin C and flavonoid content from kumquat peel; added sugar should be limited to avoid contributing to dyslipidaemia.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kumquat and lemon drink (金桔柠檬, Jin Ju Ning Meng) is a popular Chinese-style cold beverage widely served at Singaporean Chinese restaurants, hawker stalls, and beverage counters. It is made by combining freshly squeezed or blended kumquat (Fortunella spp.) with lemon juice, water, and typically a small amount of sugar or honey, served chilled. The drink has a bright, tangy-sweet flavour profile with the characteristic bittersweet notes of kumquat peel balanced by the sharp acidity of lemon. The kumquat is often used whole or with peel, contributing dietary fibre, flavonoids, and vitamin C. Per 100 ml, the ready-to-drink beverage provides approximately 38 kcal, 9.2 g carbohydrate (predominantly sugars), 0.4 g protein, and 0.1 g fat, with a meaningful vitamin C content of ~18 mg/100 ml. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 40 (low category), making it a relatively moderate-impact fruit beverage when sugar is controlled. Clinically, it is low in sodium and saturated fat, making it broadly suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management, though individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request reduced sugar versions and moderate their portion size. The drink is vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly listed on menus as 'Kumquat Lemon', '金桔柠檬', or 'Jin Ju Ning Meng' and is a staple beverage at Lanzhou beef noodle restaurants and Chinese casual dining establishments in Singapore.
common_user_queries: What is kumquat and lemon drink?
How many calories in kumquat lemon drink?
Is kumquat lemon drink good for diabetics?
What is Jin Ju Ning Meng?
Is kumquat lemon drink high in sugar?
Can I drink kumquat lemon if I have high blood pressure?
What nutrients are in kumquat lemon juice?
What is the GI of kumquat lemon drink?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for kumquat-lemon drink found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a composite beverage. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central raw ingredient data for kumquats and lemon juice with a standard dilution model. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database citrus beverage data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from 'Grains and staples' (input) to 'Beverages' — kumquat and lemon is a fruit-based cold drink, not a grain or staple. 2. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central raw ingredient data (kumquats FDC 168162, lemon juice FDC 167747) blended at approximate proportions for a ready-to-drink beverage; values should be updated if a direct HPB Singapore Foodo or equivalent entry becomes available. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based fruit beverage containing no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as vitamin D is not present in citrus fruit beverages; naturally zero. 5. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are 0.0 — naturally zero for a fruit-based beverage with no added fats or animal products; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central ingredient data. 6. GI set to 40 (low category) as an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database citrus beverage data; glycemic_load_per_serving of 9.2 calculated for a 250 ml serving: (40 × 9.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 9.2. 7. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type throughout as no specific kumquat-lemon drink codes exist in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT. 8. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean Chinese cuisine category: English (food_name), Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi. 9. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the USDA FoodData Central blended estimate model used; however, all mandatory secondary nutrients (dietary_fibre_g, sodium_mg, saturated_fat_g, sugar_g) are populated with numeric values.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_group from 'Grains and staples' to 'Beverages' — kumquat and lemon is a beverage, not a grain.
Added canonical food_name 'Kumquat and Lemon Drink' with full multilingual local_names for Singaporean Chinese cuisine (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Estimated per_100g nutrients from USDA FoodData Central raw ingredient data with transparent blending methodology.
Set glycemic_index to 40 (numeric >0, low category) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database citrus beverage data.
Used only closest_parent match_type values for all ontology fields — no invented codes.
Documented all zero values (vitamin_b12, vitamin_d, saturated_fat, etc.) as naturally zero or confirmed zero in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent — all language-specific strings present verbatim in flat aliases array.
